input.background: Refer to Example 9.10. Suppose the same factorial experiment is performed on four other brands (E, F, G, and H), and the results are as shown in Table 9.16. Repeat the factorial analysis and interpret the results.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Table 9.16 & \multicolumn{5}{|r|}{Distance Data for Second Factorial Golf Experiment} \\
\hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{} & \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{Brand} \\
\hline & & E & F & G & H \\
\hline Club & Driver & 238.6 & 261.4 & 264.7 & 235.4 \\
\hline & & 241.9 & 261.3 & 262.9 & 239.8 \\
\hline & & 236.6 & 254.0 & 253.5 & 236.2 \\
\hline & & 244.9 & 259.9 & 255.6 & 237.5 \\
\hline & & & & & \\
\hline & Five-Iron & 165.2 & 179.2 & 189.0 & 171.4 \\
\hline & & 156.9 & 171.0 & 191.2 & 159.3 \\
\hline & & 172.2 & 178.0 & 191.3 & 156.6 \\
\hline & & 163.2 & 182.7 & 180.5 & 157.4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Conduct a test for the equality of treatment means using the given data. What is the test statistic, and what conclusion can be drawn?

output.answer: The Sum of Squares for Treatments is calculated as SS(Treatments) = SS(Clubs) + SS(Brands) + SS(Interaction) = 46,443.9 + 3,410.3 + 105.2 = 49,959.4. The Mean Square for Treatments is MS(Treatments) = SS(Treatments) / (8 - 1) = 49,959.4 / 7 = 7,137.1. The test statistic is F = MS(Treatments) / MSE = 7,137.1 / 24.6 = 290.1. Since this F-value exceeds the critical value of F(10) = 1.98, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that at least two of the Brand-Club combinations have significantly different mean distances.
